nativeRange.habitat: Fast-flowing, highly oxygenated rocky river sections with clear, warm, soft acidic water; found clinging to boulders and rock faces in rapids and riffles

decorPreferences: large smooth boulders and rock stacks for hiding and territory
caves and crevices (ceramic cave tubes, coconut shells, or slate stacks)
driftwood as additional cover and biofilm surface
strong water flow (powerhead or wavemaker) to replicate Orinoco rapids

diet.notes: The Blue Phantom Pleco is a carnivore-leaning omnivore and should not be confused with algae-grazing pleco species such as Pterygoplichthys. Its diet centers on protein-rich sinking foods — high-quality carnivore pellets, frozen or freeze-dried bloodworms, brine shrimp, and mysis shrimp. It will also graze naturally occurring biofilm and aufwuchs (the thin layer of algae and microorganisms that accumulate on rock surfaces), and accepts occasional vegetable matter such as zucchini, cucumber, or blanched spinach, though these should not form the bulk of its diet. Driftwood in the aquarium provides supplemental fiber and supports digestion. This species does NOT effectively control nuisance algae and should not be kept for that purpose.

goodWith: mid-water and upper-level tetras (e.g. cardinal tetras, rummy-nose tetras)
small peaceful cichlids sharing similar water parameters (e.g. Apistogramma spp.)
other Loricariids occupying different territories (with adequate cave space)
avoidWith: other plecos in confined tanks without multiple dedicated cave territories
large aggressive cichlids that may outcompete at feeding time
fish requiring alkaline hard water (incompatible parameters)
very small nano fish that may be harassed
Green Phantom Pleco (L200) in small tanks — conspecific-style territorial conflict

summary: The Blue Phantom Pleco (L128) is an undescribed Loricariid catfish from Venezuela’s fast-flowing Orinoco tributaries, recognizable by its dark body patterned with iridescent blue-white spots. Unlike many plecos, it is a carnivore-leaning omnivore that does not graze algae and requires protein-rich foods, strong current, and rocky cave hiding spots. Intermediate care level.
careNotes:
The Blue Phantom Pleco (L128) is an undescribed species of Hemiancistrus native to the upper Río Orinoco drainage in Venezuela, primarily associated with the Río Cuao and surrounding fast-water tributaries. It is closely related to the Green Phantom Pleco (L200) and other 'Phantom' plecos within the Hemiancistrus complex. Unlike the large common plecos of the Pterygoplichthys genus that are widely sold as algae controllers, the Blue Phantom does not effectively graze nuisance algae and should not be purchased for that purpose. Its diet should center on protein-rich sinking foods — high-quality carnivore pellets, frozen bloodworms, brine shrimp, and mysis shrimp — supplemented with occasional blanched vegetables and biofilm grazed from rocks and driftwood.

Replicating the conditions of Orinoco rapids is important for long-term health. Water should be warm (26–30°C), soft (gH 2–12), slightly acidic to neutral (pH 6.0–7.2), and well-oxygenated with strong directional flow from a powerhead or wavemaker. This species is sensitive to nitrate accumulation and dissolved organic buildup, so water quality must be kept pristine; weekly partial water changes of 25–30% are generally recommended.

Territorial aggression between conspecifics and similarly built Loricariids tends to be pronounced. A single specimen should be given at least two or three cave-style hiding spots — ceramic cave tubes, slate caves, or coconut shells — so it can establish a secure territory. Males are particularly intolerant of rival males; if keeping more than one specimen, a significantly larger tank (300+ L) with multiple non-overlapping cave territories is required.

Most specimens in the trade are wild-caught, as captive breeding remains uncommon. Wild-caught individuals may require an extended acclimatisation period and tend to be more fragile than fish settled through a responsible specialist supplier. When captive-bred specimens can be sourced, they are generally considered hardier and more disease-resistant than their wild-caught counterparts. The trade designation L128 is an 'L-number' — a code assigned by hobbyist publications to undescribed pleco species before formal scientific naming. The species remains scientifically undescribed as of 2026.
breedingNotes: Breeding is possible in well-maintained aquaria and has been achieved by specialist keepers, though it is not common. The Blue Phantom Pleco is a cave spawner: the male selects and defends a cave, and the female deposits adhesive eggs inside. The male then fans and guards the clutch through hatching, which typically occurs within 5–7 days at 28°C. Fry are relatively large at hatching and can accept finely crushed sinking pellets and baby brine shrimp within a few days of emerging. Conditioning the pair with live and frozen protein-rich foods, combined with regular cool-water changes to simulate the onset of the rainy season, is generally reported to help trigger spawning. A dedicated breeding tank with soft, acidic water, strong flow, and multiple cave options tends to improve success rates. Breeding difficulty is considered moderate to difficult, owing to the specific conditioning requirements involved.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Hemiancistrus sp. (L128)
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided native range (Rio Orinoco drainage, Venezuela), water parameters, adult size (~20 cm), diet (carnivore-leaning omnivore, biofilm and meaty foods), tank requirements (strong flow, rocky caves, 200+ L), territorial behavior, and breeding notes.

additional.name: FishBase — Hemiancistrus sp.
additional.notes: Cross-referenced taxonomy (Loricariidae, Siluriformes), native range confirmation (Orinoco basin, Venezuela), and size data. L-number undescribed species; FishBase entry reflects genus-level data supplemented by L128-specific hobbyist records.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Pleco Care Guide
additional.notes: Used for trade-level care context: feeding guidance emphasizing protein-rich diet for carnivore-leaning plecos, cave requirements, water flow needs, and confirmation that L128 is not an algae grazer. General Loricariid husbandry principles applied.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — L-number Pleco Guide
additional.notes: Referenced for L128 trade designation context, relationship to L200 Green Phantom Pleco, Orinoco rapids biotope requirements, and increasing captive-bred availability from specialist breeders. Confirmed carnivore-leaning diet and territorial cave-dwelling behavior.

additional.name: PlanetCatfish — Hemiancistrus sp. (L128)
additional.notes: Specialist catfish database. Confirmed L128 designation, Venezuelan Orinoco origin (Río Cuao and nearby drainages), soft acidic warm water requirements, cave-spawning breeding behavior, and hobbyist reports of successful captive breeding. Cross-checked adult size (15–20 cm), water parameters, and carnivore-leaning diet.

fish.breedingNotes: Cave spawner in which the male selects, defends, and fans the egg clutch through hatching. Conditioning the pair with live and frozen protein-rich foods and performing cool-water changes to mimic the rainy season is generally reported to help trigger spawning. Fry accept finely crushed sinking pellets and baby brine shrimp shortly after hatching. Captive breeding has been reported by specialist keepers but is not routine.
